stem: A 26-year-old man with first-episode schizophrenia was commenced on olanzapine 10 mg nocte 11 weeks ago. He is being reviewed at the community mental health clinic. His positive symptoms have improved markedly. He has gained 5 kg since starting olanzapine. He reports increased appetite and lethargy but no new physical symptoms. He has no personal or family history of cardiometabolic disease. He smokes 10 cigarettes daily and is not physically active.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 84, RR 16, BP 128/82 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: BMI 28 kg/m^2 (up from 26.5), no acanthosis, no tremor or rigidity, mental state stable
data_table.Investigations: Baseline pre-treatment - fasting BSL 4.8 mmol/L, total cholesterol 4.2 mmol/L, ECG QTc 410 ms

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate set of monitoring tests at this 12-week review?

options.text: Liver function tests, thyroid function and serum creatinine only as the 12-week metabolic screen
options.rationale: LFTs, TFTs and creatinine are baseline and yearly tests rather than the 12-week metabolic screen, which focuses on glucose, lipids, weight and BP. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Serum prolactin and bone densitometry as the 12-week metabolic screen for second-generation antipsychotics
options.rationale: Olanzapine has relatively low prolactin-raising effect; the 12-week screen is metabolic and DEXA is not part of routine 12-week monitoring. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Annual ECG with QTc, clozapine level and full blood examination as the 12-week monitoring panel
options.rationale: Clozapine levels are irrelevant on olanzapine; FBC is mandatory for clozapine but not for olanzapine, and annual ECG is not the 12-week tool. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Fasting glucose, lipid profile, weight, waist circumference and blood pressure as the 12-week metabolic screen
options.rationale: Australian RANZCP guidance recommends a fasting metabolic screen (glucose, lipids, weight, waist circumference, BP) at 12 weeks after starting an antipsychotic, then 6-monthly. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Symptom rating scale (PANSS) and a Simpson-Angus scale with no metabolic blood tests required at 12 weeks
options.rationale: Symptom and EPS rating are important but do not substitute for metabolic blood and anthropometric monitoring which is the highest yield at 12 weeks. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Second-generation antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and clozapine, cause weight gain, dyslipidaemia and impaired glucose metabolism. Australian RANZCP guidance schedules a metabolic screen at baseline, 12 weeks and 6-monthly comprising fasting glucose, lipid profile, weight, waist circumference and BP. Early identification at 12 weeks allows lifestyle intervention or antipsychotic switch before sustained metabolic disease develops.
key_points: 12-week metabolic screen on antipsychotic - fasting glucose, lipids, weight, waist, BP
Olanzapine and clozapine have the highest cardiometabolic burden
Weight gain over 5 percent in 12 weeks should prompt lifestyle and medication review
explanation.pearls: Metformin can be added to mitigate antipsychotic-induced weight gain (around 3 kg average effect) when lifestyle measures are insufficient and switching is undesired.

stem: A 24-year-old woman known to the community mental health team for borderline personality disorder presents to ED after superficial cutting of her forearm following an argument with her partner. She has had multiple similar presentations and is engaged in weekly dialectical behaviour therapy with her usual psychologist. She denies suicidal intent and asks to go home. She has no medical injury requiring suturing. She is calm at interview and has a safety plan she co-developed with her DBT therapist.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 82, RR 16, BP 118/72 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Superficial linear lacerations on left forearm, no deep injury, no signs of intoxication, mental state - distressed but engaged, mood reactive, no psychotic features

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate ED management?

options.text: Commence a low-dose quetiapine 25 mg as required regimen for her presentation today and on discharge
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy has limited evidence in BPD and as-needed sedative-antipsychotic use risks dependence; first-line management is psychological. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Admit to the inpatient psychiatry ward overnight for crisis stabilisation and review by the on-call team in the morning
options.rationale: Routine admission for self-harm in BPD often reinforces help-seeking via crisis and disrupts therapeutic work; admission is reserved for acute psychiatric emergency or comorbid acute condition. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Validate her distress, review her safety plan with her, dress the wounds and discharge to her usual DBT clinician with rapid follow-up
options.rationale: Evidence-based BPD care prioritises continuity with the primary therapist, validation, brief crisis interventions and use of the patient's existing safety plan; routine hospital admission for self-harm in BPD does not improve outcomes. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Make her an involuntary patient under the Mental Health Act because of her history of repeated self-harm
options.rationale: BPD without acute serious risk does not warrant involuntary admission; her presentation is non-life-threatening and she has insight, capacity and a safety plan. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Discharge without follow-up because she is engaged in ongoing private psychology and has no acute medical injury
options.rationale: Discharge is appropriate but should be coordinated with the usual treating team and include rapid follow-up rather than disengaging from acute care. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

explanation.summary: Australian NHMRC guidance for borderline personality disorder prioritises a structured, evidence-based psychological therapy (DBT, MBT, schema therapy) with continuity of care. Acute presentations with non-life-threatening self-harm are managed by validating distress, supporting the patient's existing safety plan, and rapid follow-up with the primary clinician. Admission and involuntary measures are reserved for acute psychiatric emergency.
key_points: Continuity with the primary therapist is the highest-leverage acute intervention
Routine inpatient admission for self-harm in BPD does not improve outcomes
Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive and lacks BPD-specific efficacy in the long-term
explanation.pearls: Brief admission of 24 to 72 hours may be useful in acute suicidal crisis to prevent imminent harm; planned and time-limited stays reduce reinforcement of crisis presentations.

stem: A 32-year-old man with a 4-year history of schizoaffective disorder is admitted to a general medical ward from a psychiatric inpatient unit because he has stopped eating, drinking and moving over 48 hours. He has been on risperidone 6 mg daily for 2 years. Physical examination shows mutism, waxy flexibility and persistent staring. He resists passive arm movement but yields once the arm is positioned. CK is 480 U/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L, urinalysis normal and there is no fever or autonomic instability.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.9 degrees Celsius, HR 86, RR 18, BP 118/74 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, eyes open and tracking, waxy flexibility on arm posturing, no fever, no diaphoresis, no rigidity, no tremor
data_table.Investigations: CK 480 U/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L, CRP 4 mg/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange MRI brain and CSF examination before any treatment to exclude autoimmune encephalitis as the cause
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis warrants investigation in atypical or refractory cases, but does not delay urgent treatment of the catatonic state; lorazepam is safe and aids diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

options.text: Cease risperidone and commence dantrolene 1 mg/kg intravenously for suspected neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS requires fever, autonomic instability and significant rigidity with a markedly elevated CK; their absence here makes catatonia more likely than NMS. (Source: RANZCP NMS Position)

options.text: Commence electroconvulsive therapy this morning without further pharmacological challenge first
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective in catatonia but is reserved for lorazepam non-response, life-threatening features (malignant catatonia) or recurrent catatonia; trial of lorazepam comes first. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

options.text: Increase risperidone to 8 mg daily for inadequate antipsychotic effect causing residual negative symptoms
options.rationale: Increasing antipsychotic dose may worsen catatonia (and could precipitate NMS); catatonia in psychosis is treated by stopping or reducing antipsychotic and adding benzodiazepine. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

options.text: Administer lorazepam 2 mg intravenously and reassess catatonic features at 10 minutes for a positive response
options.rationale: A lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV) is the diagnostic and first-line treatment step for catatonia; partial or full response within minutes confirms the diagnosis and guides further benzodiazepine dosing. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

explanation.summary: Catatonia presents with mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility and posturing and may complicate psychotic or mood disorders. The lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV with reassessment at 5 to 10 minutes) is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Lack of fever, autonomic instability and severe rigidity argues against NMS. ECT is reserved for benzodiazepine non-responders, malignant or recurrent catatonia.
key_points: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is first-line diagnostic and therapeutic test for catatonia
NMS requires fever, autonomic instability and significant rigidity with marked CK rise
ECT is reserved for non-response, malignant or recurrent catatonia
explanation.pearls: Excited catatonia (hyperkinetic variant) presents with frenzied motor activity rather than stupor; lorazepam still works but the differential broadens to include serotonin syndrome and acute psychosis.

stem: A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to ED with 24 hours of fever, dysuria and right flank pain. He is alert but tachycardic. Initial obs show HR 124, BP 88/52 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 96% on room air, temp 39.2 degrees Celsius. A bedside lactate is 3.4 mmol/L. He has no allergies. Renal ultrasound is being arranged. You are the receiving registrar in the resuscitation bay deciding the hour-1 plan.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.2 degrees Celsius, HR 124, RR 28, BP 88/52 mmHg, SpO2 96% in room air, GCS 15
data_table.Examination: Warm peripheries, capillary refill 3 seconds, right renal angle tenderness, no peritonism, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 3.4 mmol/L, WCC 18 x10^9/L, CRP 220 mg/L, creatinine 156 micromol/L (baseline 90), urinalysis 3+ leukocytes 2+ nitrites, two blood cultures sent

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate hour-1 sepsis bundle?

options.text: Cautious 500 mL crystalloid bolus, intravenous ceftriaxone and start noradrenaline if MAP remains under 65 mmHg
options.rationale: 30 mL/kg crystalloid is the bundle standard in septic shock before vasopressor consideration; restricting to 500 mL initially is inadequate resuscitation in this group. (Source: ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 750 mg twice daily and 10 mL/kg of crystalloid prior to admission to a medical ward
options.rationale: Septic shock with hypotension and elevated lactate mandates IV antibiotics within an hour and 30 mL/kg fluid resuscitation, not oral monotherapy; ciprofloxacin alone has resistance concerns in urinary sepsis. (Source: ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

options.text: Crystalloid 30 mL/kg over the first hour, intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam after cultures and serial lactate
options.rationale: Australian sepsis pathways follow the hour-1 bundle of fluid 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 2 mmol/L, blood cultures before broad-spectrum antibiotics targeted at source, and reassessment with serial lactate. Piperacillin-tazobactam covers urinary sepsis with possible Pseudomonas. (Source: ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

options.text: Activated protein C infusion, intravenous gentamicin alone and hydrocortisone 200 mg as adjunctive therapy
options.rationale: Activated protein C has been withdrawn from sepsis management; gentamicin alone does not cover for resistant Gram-negatives, and routine hydrocortisone is reserved for refractory shock. (Source: ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

options.text: Delay antibiotics until renal ultrasound has excluded obstructive uropathy and surgical drainage is planned
options.rationale: Antibiotic delay is associated with increased mortality in sepsis; ultrasound is performed in parallel and drainage arranged urgently without delaying empirical antibiotics. (Source: ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

explanation.summary: Septic shock with hypotension and raised lactate is managed under the hour-1 bundle. The bundle comprises blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics targeted at the suspected source, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 2 mmol/L, and reassessment with repeat lactate. Source control imaging is arranged in parallel rather than as a reason to delay therapy. Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor when fluid resuscitation alone fails to maintain mean arterial pressure of 65 mmHg.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle - cultures, antibiotics, fluid 30 mL/kg, repeat lactate
Piperacillin-tazobactam covers urinary sepsis with possible Pseudomonas risk
Source control imaging occurs in parallel and never delays empirical antibiotics
explanation.pearls: Patients with septic shock requiring vasopressors should have a target MAP of 65 mmHg; noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor and is started after adequate fluid resuscitation.

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to ED with 3 days of cough, fever and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. She lives independently and is non-smoker. She has hypertension only. CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation. She is well perfused and orientated.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4 degrees Celsius, HR 96, RR 24, BP 124/76 mmHg, SpO2 92% on room air, GCS 15
data_table.Examination: Coarse crackles right base, no wheeze, no peripheral oedema, orientated to time and place
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14 x10^9/L, urea 6.8 mmol/L, CRP 188 mg/L, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, multifocal albumin 36 g/L, ABG pH 7.42 PaO2 68 mmHg on room air, blood cultures sent

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate disposition and antibiotic plan?

options.text: Admit for IV moxifloxacin monotherapy as appropriate cover for atypical and typical bacteria in CAP
options.rationale: Quinolone monotherapy is reserved for penicillin allergy or specific situations; first-line for moderate CAP is benzylpenicillin plus doxycycline or ceftriaxone plus doxycycline. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Community-acquired pneumonia)

options.text: Discharge on oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily and oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Hypoxia with SpO2 92 percent on room air pushes severity to moderate and warrants admission rather than outpatient management. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Community-acquired pneumonia)

options.text: Admit to ICU for intravenous ceftriaxone, azithromycin and noradrenaline support as severe CAP
options.rationale: She does not meet ICU CAP criteria - no shock, no respiratory failure requiring intubation or NIV, no multilobar progression at presentation. ICU admission is over-treatment. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Community-acquired pneumonia)

options.text: Admit for IV benzylpenicillin and oral doxycycline using the moderate-severity community-acquired pneumonia regimen
options.rationale: SMART-COP of 3 (RR 24 - 1 point, SpO2 92 percent - 2 points; albumin 36 g/L is not low enough; urea 6.8 mmol/L is borderline; otherwise 0) indicates moderate severity requiring admission; Australian first-line for moderate CAP is IV benzylpenicillin plus oral doxycycline. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Community-acquired pneumonia)

options.text: Admit for IV vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam covering hospital-acquired and aspiration pathogens
options.rationale: This is community-acquired pneumonia in a community-dwelling woman without aspiration risk; antipseudomonal and MRSA cover is not warranted. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Community-acquired pneumonia)

explanation.summary: SMART-COP stratifies community-acquired pneumonia severity. A score of 3 indicates moderate severity requiring admission. Australian first-line therapy for moderate CAP is intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6-hourly plus oral doxycycline 100 mg BD. Severe disease (score 5 or higher, ICU need) escalates to IV ceftriaxone plus IV azithromycin. Oral amoxicillin plus doxycycline is appropriate for mild outpatient CAP.
key_points: SMART-COP 0-2 outpatient; 3-4 ward; 5 or higher consider ICU
Moderate CAP - IV benzylpenicillin plus oral doxycycline first-line in Australia
Hypoxia (SpO2 under 93 percent on room air) is a major driver of admission decisions
explanation.pearls: Re-evaluate at 48 to 72 hours; non-responders need workup for organising pneumonia, empyema, resistant organism (Legionella, MRSA) and aspiration; CT chest is informative.
